paper_title: TdeA, a TolC-like protein required for toxin and drug export in Aggregatibacter (Actinobacillus) actinomycetemcomitans
verbatim_quote: As part of its array of virulence factors, A. actinomycetemcomitans produces leukotoxin (LtxA), a member of the RTX family of toxins. ... In other RTX toxin-producing bacteria, an outer membrane channel-forming protein, TolC, is required for toxin secretion and drug export. ... Inactivation of this ORF resulted in a mutant that was no longer beta-hemolytic and did not secrete LtxA. ... These results show that A. actinomycetemcomitans indeed requires a TolC-like protein for LtxA secretion and that this protein, TdeA, also functions as part of a drug efflux system.

The audit note pointed to Lally 1989 (BBRC, PMID 2647082, DOI 10.1016/0006-291x(89)92431-5), which I confirmed resolves; however that paper only cloned/identified the ltxA gene and did NOT experimentally establish the T1SS secretion route. The RTX T1SS exporter for this toxin is LtxB (ABC transporter) + LtxD (MFP) + TdeA (TolC-like OM channel). I confirmed the corrected DOI resolves via Crossref to the exact title/journal/authors. Family-level support (LktA/RTX as validated T1SS substrates) is independently corroborated by Linhartova 2010 (10.1111/j.1574-6976.2010.00231.x) per the audit note.

The predicted amino acid sequences of these B. cepacia and a B. pseudomallei extracellular zinc metalloproteases indicate that they are similar to the thermolysin-like family of metalloproteases (M4 family of metalloendopeptidases) and they are likely to be secreted via the general secretory pathway.

The audit's suggested replacement (Kooi/Corbett/Sokol 2005, 10.1128/JB.187.13.4421-4429.2005, PMID 15968051) is a real paper but covers ZmpA's enzymatic/active-site function, not its secretion route. The correct primary reference for ZmpA secretion is Corbett, Burtnick, Kooi, Woods, Sokol 2003, "An extracellular zinc metalloprotease gene of Burkholderia cepacia," Microbiology 149:2263-2271, DOI 10.1099/mic.0.26243-0 (note: .26243-0, one digit family off from the broken .27353-0). This paper experimentally established ZmpA as an EXTRACELLULAR (secreted) protease: it cloned zmpA, made isogenic insertional mutants in B. cepacia genomovar III (= B. cenocepacia) strains, and showed reduced extracellular protease. The secretion ROUTE is assigned to the general secretory pathway (Sec/T2SS main terminal branch), but the abstract states this as a sequence-based inference ("likely to be secreted via the general secretory pathway") rather than a direct T2SS-dependence experiment (e.g. a gsp/xcp mutant abolishing ZmpA secretion). Hence status RESOLVED for "experimentally secreted/extracellular substrate" but confidence is medium on the specific T2SS-route assignment, which rests on M4-family homology and the extracellular phenotype rather than a translocation-mutant experiment. UniProt Q7WSN3 (BCAL2284, B. cenocepacia J2315) is the zmpA gene product. According to PubMed.

paper_title: Agrobacterium rhizogenes GALLS protein contains domains for ATP binding, nuclear localization, and type IV secretion.
verbatim_quote: Using GFP as a read-out for translocation, we found that the Cre protein fused to the C-terminal 27 amino acids of GALLS (Cre::GALLS-27) was transported efficiently from A. tumefaciens LBA1100 (vir wt) into plant cells [...] No GFP expression was detected using this virD4 mutant, indicating that GALLS translocation requires an intact VirB/D4 system.
quote_source: Body (Results/Discussion), PMC full text PMC1698208, DOI 10.1128/JB.00747-06
reasoning: The stored DOI 10.1094/MPMI-19-1145 returns HTTP 404 at Crossref and is a wrong-journal artifact (MPMI), not the real reference. The GALLS literature is in J Bacteriol. The primary experimental paper demonstrating T4SS translocation is Hodges et al. 2006, J Bacteriol 188(23):8222-30, DOI 10.1128/JB.00747-06 (PMID 17012398, PMC1698208), confirmed to resolve via Crossref. It used a CRAfT (Cre recombinase translocation) assay to directly show the Cre::GALLS-27 C-terminal fusion was transported from A. tumefaciens into plant cells, and that translocation requires an intact VirB/D4 type IV secretion system (abolished in a virD4 mutant). This is direct experimental evidence that GALLS is a T4SS substrate, matching the assigned label. The follow-up 2008 paper (DOI 10.1128/JB.01018-08, title 'GALLS Gene Encodes Two Secreted Proteins') corroborates secretion. Biology label (T4SS substrate, A. rhizogenes, VirE2 functional analog) is correct. No UniProt/locus_tag available in source. paper_title: Interaction with CagF is required for translocation of CagA into the host via the Helicobacter pylori type IV secretion system.
verbatim_quote: Here we demonstrate that CagA, in addition to other yet-unidentified proteins, interacts with CagF, presumably at the inner bacterial membrane. This interaction is required for CagA translocation, since an isogenic nonpolar cagF mutant was translocation deficient. Our results suggest that CagF may be a protein with unique chaperone-like function that is involved in the early steps of CagA recognition and delivery into the type IV secretion channel.
reasoning: UniProt O25276 maps correctly to HP_0543 = CagF (Cag22 / Cag pathogenicity island protein) in H. pylori 26695; the audit note's O25257/HP_0520 mismatch concerned a different accession and does not apply here, so gene/locus identity is sound. The stored primary DOI (10.1074/jbc.M501633200) is a JBC accession that does not correspond to a CagF paper. The two primary experimental papers on CagF (Couturier et al. 2006, Infect Immun, 10.1128/IAI.74.1.273-281.2006; Pattis et al. 2007, Microbiology, 10.1099/mic.0.2007/007385-0) both establish that CagF is a type IV secretion CHAPERONE-LIKE protein that binds CagA near its C-terminal secretion signal at the bacterial cytoplasmic/inner membrane and is REQUIRED for translocation of the CagA effector, but is NOT itself translocated into the host cell. This contradicts the benchmark label that CagF is a secreted substrate/cargo of the T4SS: CagF is machinery (a cytoplasmic-membrane chaperone), and CagA is the translocated effector. Pattis 2007 explicitly names CagA as 'the CagA effector protein' and CagF as 'a secretion chaperone-like protein.' Both DOIs were confirmed via Crossref to resolve to these exact papers. Status=CONTRADICTED because no experimental evidence shows CagF itself is secreted/translocated; the evidence shows the opposite (it functions inside the bacterium to deliver CagA).
